item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait… there wasn't one?
BC I could have SWORN I heard someone say "Hello?" while I was reading through the interview transcript.
Jeebus jumpin' on a cracker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't go in the basement.

item.title: Fantastic!
item.content:encoded:
"there were definitely ten." I was creeped out the entire time (pretty rare thing for me with SCPs) but that ending is gonna fricking stick with me for quite some time.
There are a few places where you repeat words (I'm not sure if you still check this or not, if so, I can be more specific), but overall, this might have just supplanted SCP-1936 "Daleport" as my favorite SCP entry so far, and I am freaking hungry for more and to find out what happened in Salvation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the heart attack. I still don't get why there's a giant pile of schools underneath; is it because, as per SCP-4833, this has happened more than once? Regardless, +1 for making me nearly shit myself if nothing else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

There was supposed to be an audio file or something…?

I mean, on the one hand, missed the impact, on the other, would have legit make me screech, so perhaps a well.

Just me (my browser maytbe, I've notoiced recently Waterfox doesnt want ot play with some sites all of a sudden) or has something changed in the article…?

+1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thank shit for the discussion section
I almost had a heart attack
you had my +1 anyways, but take it with extra spice now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article so much, both for the nostalgia theme, and also being from a small city (not as small as Salvation but still) all of the things in the theme struck a chord with me, the final reveal of the interview with Ms. Fletcher about the identity of the nine figures got me all mindblown if I'm honest.

+1 for all the creepiness, I also took care of the translation in the Spanish wiki in case you want to check it, that's how much I loved it too.

item.title: Re: Why are you using the british "torch"?
item.content:encoded: Please don't. I call it a torch, and I wrote the article.
okay, you got me. halfway through the second log and you got me. kudos!

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: IRC. Information here: http://www.scp-wiki.net/chat-guide

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: What chat? And I did send them a PM

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Send them a wikidot PM, and/or try to find them in chat.

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Wow. That was fast. How do I go about contacting the author?

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
In american English a torch is a burning piece of wood like you'd find in Skyrim. Plus the SCP takes place in America. The article would have been written by an american.

In this case, it's the author's stylistic choice, similar to with UK spelling variants.

I've taken the liberty of changing every instance of "torch" to flashlight to add much needed readability and accuracy.

Please don't do this without authorial permission. The edit has been reverted, and can be reinstated with the author's go-ahead.

item.title: Why are you using the british "torch"?
item.content:encoded:
It's extremely confusing and bizarre. In american English a torch is a burning piece of wood like you'd find in Skyrim. Plus the SCP takes place in America. The article would have been written by an american. It's entirely ridiculous and needlessly confusing to say "torch". I've taken the liberty of changing every instance of "torch" to flashlight to add much needed readability and accuracy.

Edit: Feel free to change it back but when I read torch, even knowing better, I couldn't help but imagine a grown man in body armor with a medieval torch (complete with open flame) tied to his shoulder. It completely took me out of the story, and I seriously doubt that a brit seeing the word flashlight would have such or strong (or even remotely similar) reaction, especially when considering that "flashlight" is much more acurate.

item.title: Classic FBI!
item.content:encoded: Real talk, i thoroughly enjoyed the UIU log. That, and it's quite in character for the FBI to mass administer a dangerous chem to the populace

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was reading this at school, and holy heck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kactus the hello sound made me fall out of my chair you goddamn bastard

But holy shit this thing scares the fuck outta me. Weird/creepy high school photos from the 70’s and before are just so unsettling for me. The whole bits about students sinking into the ground and screaming for help while everyone simply looks at you, the cheerleading team fiasco, the people rolling their eyes into the backs of their head and especially the whole part with the teleportation into the ground shit REALLY helped push the case that something demonic and broken is going down.

the kid drowned in the pool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amazing! Creepy as all heck, really brings out a theme of something incomprehensible.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, has a good atmosphere.

Not a fan of the whole "exploration team vanishes, but their radios reactivate sometimes in order to say creepy shit" trope mostly because Exploration Log 455-3 kind of did the most possible with that format. The fragments that we hear do contain important plot exposition to help readers piece together the SCP ("the tenth is down below", etc) but I feel that could have been conveyed more effectively without using an exploration log.

I like that we don't see the tenth cheerleader at all, only the traumatic cheerleader-shaped wound in Salvation she left behind. I also really like the line "It is currently believed that original inhabitants of Salvation are unable to reproduce." which sneaks up on you after you've read the final interview. I like that we don't ever find out the source of the anomaly.

Audio file probably would have been scarier if I wasn't also playing music through my speakers at the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Came back to reread, got the shit scared out of me by the audio.

I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That "hello" scared the absolute shit out of me.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I added the class-of-76 tag given the year of the anomaly and the "Syncope" reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just about pissed myself when I heard "hello"

Take my +1, you monster.

item.title: Re: Class of 76?
item.content:encoded: And the "Syncope" satchel, and bodies in the water. Omg this is a Class-of-76 article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s strange to say this for an article from this author, but this is underrated.

Nitpicks
– hide block
  • “…is seen rolling their eyes back in their head… They reappear…” (lines describing the unknown student who falls through the floor) in sentences immediately after, the article uses “he”.
  • In the PoI interview, Mrs. Fletcher says “We did have a child disappear once,” and then she goes on to describe multiple missing children (“nobody ever saw them again,” “they just ran away”, “some of them were problem children”). Since this discrepancy isn’t addressed by Agent Aimes, it makes me think it’s unintended. But, CHEMICAL 110.
Praise
– hide block
  • The subtleties of the anomalous phenomena described in the UIU report are absolutely chilling. There’s just enough measure of the illogical; these phenomena could easily be overdone in the hands of a less-skilled author.
  • The repetitive theme of nines is effective, I especially liked the repetition of silence for nine and multiples of it.
  • That Mrs. Fletcher constantly says responds “hello?” is great.
  • There’s a lot going on here and on a first read, it can seem disjointed. After reading it a second time and with help from the declassified article (see comment above), there is still a small bit of adipose tissue to the narrative imo, but it can all be connected convincingly. I think the way the author has laid out all the pieces to be connected, without doing the work for us, but giving us a sufficient nudge to do so, is the most impressive part of this article. The dark tangle of what lies beneath the article (in an all-too-fitting analogy with the anomaly itself) is what ultimately makes this not just a really really good article, but a beautiful one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such a good article. A perfect, eerie vibe, signature of djKaktus. And it makes an even better story when you couple it with its declassification. As djKaktus is still active, I hope to see more articles like this ^_^ +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is already creepy af and then you people add those "hello"s. Thanks I died. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately the hello thing didn't work for me. On mobile though, might be why. Kinda a shame that I missed that. Still, great piece otherwise, love these kinds of mysteries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see a monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entire student body shows up for school on Friday.

As a teacher in small-town Indiana, I can say this is the most unbelievable part of the the whole SCiP. Creepy Shadows? Sure. Students falling through the floor? Poor budgets. All the students show up for school? On a Friday? Unbelievable.

The interior of the school has become non-Euclidean.

The interior of most schools would be non-Euclidean already. Are you saying the school was anomalously completely Euclidean before?

item.title: Re: I Hope You're Proud Of What You've Done
item.content:encoded: I legitimately cannot take credit for this. Cimmerian was the mastermind behind that tomfuckery, I just approved its addition. It has worked exactly as we intended, though.

item.title: I Hope You're Proud Of What You've Done
item.content:encoded:
I left this tab open intending to read it, but then when to chat on IRC. I had my headphones on ofc, but my play list had finished and I hadn't queued another set, so it was all quiet.

And then what you you know I hear "hello" in my right ear and I nearly jump out of my chair and take my headphones off. My roommates give me this strange look and I say

"One of you is screwing with me, right?"

And then I proceed to interrogate my roommates for a little while. After they've convinced me I'm hearing things (quite fitting for this piece in retrospect), I put my headphones on. Lo and behold, just a little while later I hear another "hello". And it is at this point that I type into IRC "my headphones have it out for me."

It is not until 5 minutes later that I actually get around to reading the article, where I come across the line:

At 7:56am, the entire school hears someone whisper the word “hello” in their right ear.

In conclusion - well played djkaktus Cimmerian. Well played. I mean I kinda played myself… but still. Well played.

Oh and the actual article was pretty amazing as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assume that this is caused by an incorporeal entity collective that manifested as the result of actions by Syncope Symphony who did it for the same reason they made the instruments for SCP-332 (Why? I'm not entirely sure) or by the death of that girl who had some latent reality-warping abilities that went out of control because of her death (and Syncope Symphony only has a minor role in all of this.).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a lot of Love Canal. Obviously non anomolous but the idea of a school and neighborhood being stricken with strange effects always creeped me out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. The timeline gives a great feeling of progression, and has the same creepy, mysterious feeling of an early season Buffy episode; you get this feeli. The cheerleaders are the kind of image that really sticks with you, and I love how the pieces of the puzzle slowly come together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First time I'm posting on the site, but GOD that is a good article. I haven't read a Kaktus piece I haven't adored, and this has to be the first scp to get a sound out of me while reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting stuff. The pictures really sell it and make me want to know more. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man.

If you happen to live in a former school, and you're really tired and having a sudden flash of anxiety, and it's about 8 PM so everyone's coming home so there's weird sounds all over the place, and you're reading 'cause you can't bring yourself to sleep yet, AND THE ANOMALY STARTS FREAKING TALKING TO YOU WHEN YOU LOOK AT IT

You are very likely to upset your cat by unexpectedly throwing a laptop at her.

(Thankfully it missed, so she's okay, BUT STILL)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one has a really interesting effect. Despite being text, it's very visual and choppy, jumping from one image to the next. Some of the imagery feels as palpably aggressive as horror vocaloid songs or Korean jump-scare webcomics. For example, the nine figures scaring off the MTF from the house was a good image.

On the downside, where it also reminds me of those short visual media is that it feels like it's constantly jumping between different, largely unrelated main stories. Both parts on the underground hell maze are easily the worst and least necessary part of this. It had some pretty great imagery, but you get off in the weeds there.

+1 I suppose. This certainly has its flaws, it doesn't entirely gel together as a skip this ambitious could, but this is risky and unique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not that well versed in scip lore. But I've come to discover that whenever pregnant girls are involved it's usually a reference to 231

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apparently so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I'm interpreting it correctly, number 10 is the black figure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit djkaktus. If the anomaly is gonna come out of the screen and speak to me I'd better get my money's worth and have it come out of the screen to instantly terminate me.

In all seriousness, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mrs. Fletcher: Well… yes, I do. Those are the, uh- that's the cheerleaders. (Pauses) There were, uh, well, let me think… there were ten of them, though, I think. Unless something happened to one of them, there were definitely ten.

But, there were 9 hanging women. What happened to girl #10?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lmao alright. Working as intended, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was reading this alone in the middle of the night. When I got to the description, I heard a "hello" that nearly gave me a heart attack. As great as this article is, I can't respect an SCP that uses a cheap jumpscare instead of words to evoke fear.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one really didn't do it for me, a lot of build up and a payoff that didn't grab me.

The descriptions of the spooky happenings felt like a write-up of an indie horror game complete with jump scares and frozen character models jerking around and clipping through walls. I sort of enjoyed how bizarre it was but I needed it to go somewhere

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When it said "hello", I nearly jumped out of my skin. That was brilliant. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3935 is an extra-temporal, extra-spatial, non-Euclidian space

But it says "Object Class: Euclid" in the article? :-/

(Just joking.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely a +1.

I love how it adds a massively creepy layer to the whole "Class of '76" thing I've fallen in love with on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was reading the article when I heard someone whisper "hello" and it scared the ever-loving shit out of me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anomalous location in North America with extraspacial properties that adheres to non-linear timespace, complete with a MTF investigation log? Gotta be a Kaktus SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great and unsettling SCP! I got chills multiple times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful skip, but shouldn't UIU be tagged here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this, but the last bit about the pregnant girl and the figure in the sky kinda threw me off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't going to upvote this one. Nothing wrong with it, it just wasn't especially grabbing me.

Then I heard something and just about had a heart attack. And I can't, in good conscience, not upvote any article that gets that sort of response out of me. Well played.

item.title: I do not recognize the bodies in the high school
item.content:encoded:
Why is it I get to see all the super-crappy skips in all their glory, even after they've been deleted, but the RSS consistently refuses to show me things like this? Knocked another one out of the park, kaktus!

The figure begins to phase in and out of the window. The figure disappears, and then begins to phase in and out of other windows.

Intentional? I was also bothered by the one remaining SCP reference in that log (also 'amnestic'), but reading back up that it was a summary explained it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got my vote. This was a lot of fun to read, like a 70's X-files episode or something. Creepy and immersive and entertaining, thanks for doing that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The music was great too.
